## What Are Claude Code Hooks?
- **Command hooks** — run a shell command (JSON on stdin, results via exit codes/stdout)

- **HTTP hooks** — POST the event's JSON to a URL
- **MCP tool hooks** — call a tool on a connected MCP server

- **Prompt hooks** — send a prompt to a Claude model for single-turn evaluation

## Key Benefit
- [Claude Code Hooks Mastery (GitHub)](https://github.com/disler/claude-code-hooks-mastery)

- [Claude Code hooks explained: the missing layer between prompts and production](https://joseparreogarcia.substack.com/p/claude-code-hooks-explained-the-missing)
- [Claude Code Hooks – DEV Community](https://dev.to/helderberto/claude-code-hooks-1k7a)
- [Claude Code now supports hooks – Hacker News](https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=44429225)
- [Hooks reference – Claude Code Docs](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/hooks)
- [karanb192/claude-code-hooks (GitHub)](https://github.com/karanb192/claude-code-hooks)

- `PostToolUse` hook to auto-run a formatter after every file edit
- `Stop`/`Notification` hook to fire a desktop notification when Claude finishes or needs input

Done. Here's what the search returned on Claude Code hooks:

Hooks are user-defined shell commands, HTTP endpoints, or LLM prompts that execute automatically at specific points in Claude Code's lifecycle — like git hooks, but for your AI agent.

## How They Work
I'll search for that.

Unlike prompt-based rules (probabilistic), hooks give **deterministic, guaranteed execution**. A `PreToolUse` hook blocking writes to `.env` files will *always* block them, whereas a CLAUDE.md instruction only "probably" will.

## Common Use Cases

When an event fires and a matcher matches, Claude Code passes JSON context about the event to your hook handler. A hook starts with an event, can optionally be narrowed with a matcher, receives structured input, then executes one or more handlers.

## Types of Hooks

Claude Code hooks are user-defined shell commands, HTTP endpoints, or LLM prompts that execute automatically at specific points in Claude Code's lifecycle — similar to git hooks, but for the AI agent. They fire on events (optionally narrowed by a matcher), receive structured JSON input from Claude Code, and run handlers. Common uses include auto-running a formatter on file edits (PostToolUse) or forcing Claude to keep working when it tries to stop (Stop).
